title: Lichen
text:
There are three primary types of lichens: Foliose (leaf-like in appearance), Crustose (crusty looking), and Fruiticose (coral-like and bushy). They are abundant in the region and are great bioindicators of air quality!
-Amanda Garner

title: Norris Dam
text: This was the first dam built by the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA), completed in 1936. It is 265 feet high and a little more than 1,800 feet wide. Behind it lies Norris Lake, a favorite for paddling and boating, while the Clinch River flows just below.

title: Pine Trees
text: A family of conifers recognizable by their needles, which usually grow in bundles, and their seeds, which develop in stiff cones—making them distinct from hemlocks, spruces, and firs. Look for pines as you hike up to the ridge.

title: Brushy Mountain State Penitentiary
text: A closed prison near Frozen Head State Park is full of lore due to its remote location. Part of the inspiration for the Barkley Marathons (you'll have to look it up). Now the site of a restaurant and distillery.

title: Timber Rattlesnake
text: The Timber rattlesnake is the most venomous snake in Tennessee.  Though potentially dangerous, this species is reclusive, shy, and would rather hide using its amazing camouflage pattern. They are our largest venomous snake sometimes exceeding 6 ft. in length. Like copperheads they also have heat sensing pits. They are identifiable by their rattle and chevron pattern. - Alex Edwards

title: Bigleaf Magnolia
text: This tree has the largest simple leaf of any North American tree—one single, undivided leaf! In the fall, its leaves scatter across the ground like pages from a newspaper. You'll find it common on the Cumberland Plateau, though it's rare in the Smokies. Search for their large leaves along this trail.

title: Eastern Copperhead
text: The copperhead is our most common of Tennessee's venomous snakes. It is a pit viper, which means it has an external organ (pits) that allow it to see the heat signatures of its prey. This along with its fantastic camouflage makes this a great ambush predator.  Identifiable by its "Hershey kiss" pattern. - Alex Edwards

title: The Provenance of Abrams Falls
text: Angel Falls is more a rapids than a waterfall (it's not very tall), but there's a history to its name: It was a waterfall until the 1940s, when it was dynamited to make the river more easily navigable by paddlers and boaters. It was only a little successful: the rapids are known as being impassable for all but the most intrepid whitewater paddlers.

title: Paw Paw
text: A native fruit found around Seven Islands. Tastes like a mix of an apple and a banana. Paw Paw flowers are pollinated by beetles and flies.

title: Sandstone
text: The Cumberland Plateau's sandstone cliffs tell their age in layers—tan and gray rock streaked with rust-orange where ancient iron has slowly rusted over millions of years. Water and time have carved dramatic overhangs called rockhouses, creating natural shelters that kids can't resist exploring. At Honey Creek, these bluffs tower overhead at nearly every turn.

title: Trillium
text:
Native perennial plants with distinctive three-petal flowers that bloom in the spring. These shade-loving plants are often found in the forest understory, where their seeds are dispersed and buried by ants, in a process called myrmecochory. Trillium plants are protected in many places because they are extremely slow-growing. For some species, it can take anywhere from 5-9 years from being planted to their first bloom; a single plant can live up to 25 years! So, next time you see one, make sure you appreciate their beauty with your eyes only.
- MaryRose Weatherton

title: Eastern Hemlock
text: A tree that feels" like the Smokies. Characterized by its needles being arranged in a flat frond. Threatened by an invasive species

title: Blue Ridge Two-Lined Salamander
text:
The Great Smoky Mountains has more than 30 species of salamanders – one of the types of species that contributes to and characterizes the incredible biodiversity of the Smokies. The Blue Ridge Two-Lined Salamander is among the most beautiful salamanders, with its yellow-to-orange coloring and stark black stripes.
- Amanda Garner

title: Brook Trout
text: The native species of trout in Smokies streams. Speckled and small. Found at high elevations, whereas the Rainbow Trout and Brown Trout were later introduced for fishing.

title: The Cherokee
text: The people of the Cherokee Nation were indigenous to a vast area to the south, east, and west of the Smokies; the Smokies were more a place for gathering and hunting than residing. The Eastern Band of the Cherokee remains an autonomous Nation just south of the Smokies.

title: Smallmouth Bass
text: The smallmouth bass is Tennessee's state fish. This sunfish species can be identified by the lines on its face and a jaw (maxilla) that does not go past the eye (smaller). They are fantastic gamefish and are often the top predator in the streams and creeks of Tennessee. - Alex Edwards

title: Fraser Fir
text: You'll feel like you're in a different state or country when you spot these trees atop the Smokies – usually at elevations above 5,500 feet. Note the Christmas tree smell! From Look Rock, you can see the ridge of the Smokies where Fraser Firs grow. Afflicted by the Balsam Wooly Adelgid and potentially a changing climate around the ridge of the Smokies.

title: Mountain Laurel
text: A large bush in the same family as Rhododendron and Blueberries - Heath plants. Beautiful flowers around early June. Can be distinguished from Rhododendron by its location: typically on ridges away from water.

title: Black Bear
text: A symbol of the wildness of the Smokies -- and a very real presence. If you see one, admire their strength, beauty, and intelligence (from a distance!). Commonly seen in nearby Cades Cove. Here is a photo of a mother bear and cubs forage near the trail — time to back away quietly

title: Jordans Red Cheek
text:
These iconic Smokies salamanders are found only in the Smokies! If you are above 2,500 ft, you might just see one of these amazing critters.
- Amanda Garner

title: Blue Phlox
text: Blue Phlox is a native wildflower that blooms in early to mid-spring. These plants provide an important source of early-season nectar for pollinators, like the swallowtail butterfly and other native moths. -MaryRose Weatherton

title: Rosebay Rhododendron
text: These plants are commonly seen on slopes near streams, which helps stabilize the soil. Looks similar to Mountain Laurel; has larger leaves. Pretty, but all parts of this plant are toxic to humans and pets! You can't miss the Rosebay Rhododendron on this hike around inspiration point.  -MaryRose Weatherton

title: Red Spruce
text: Similar to Fraser Fir, but larger (taller and wider) and found at lower elevations (beginning around 4,000 feet). Majestic. When you see them, you feel like you're in high and remote mountains.

title: Appalachian Trail
text: Surprisingly, the crest of the Smokies (and the Appalachian Trail) are visible from this park. The Appalachian Trail is more than 2,100 miles long, beginning at Springer Mountain, Georgia, and ending at Mount Katahdin, in Maine. The longest section without a road crossing is in the Smokies – from Fontana Dam to Newfound Gap.

title: Eastern Bluebird
text: While many birds stop over at Seven Islands and areas like that on their migratory routes south (in the fall) and north (in the spring), the Eastern Bluebird is a year-round resident. Bluebirds are obligate cavity nesters - which is why they have experienced such a decline in their population as urban sprawl has increased. Refuges like Seven Islands give them lots of opportunities to find natural cavities to nest in! -MaryRose Weatherton

title: Bald Eagle
text: Common along local rivers, including the river beside this hike---the Tennessee River. You may even spot a nesting pair on nearby Looney Island.

title: Elm Tree
text: Trees characterized by an umbrella-shaped crown. The tree near the start of this hike (surrounded by a wire enclosure to prevent cars from being parked nearby) is known as a Champion" of its kind – the largest Elm in Knoxville."

title: Native American Mounds
text: These earthen mounds are found in many places along the Mississippi River and its tributaries—like the Tennessee River. Nobody is completely sure why they were built, but some believe they served as gathering spots or markers to return to time and again. The mound here may date back as early as the 1400s, likely created by one of the Native Nations with ancestral ties to the Knoxville region.

title: Tennessee Marble
text: Mead's Quarry–near the parking area now filled with crystal clear water–was a quarry for what was called Tennessee Marble". Geologically

title: Eastern Box Turtle
text: Box turtles have the unique ability to completely hide in their shells due to a hinged plastron. They are long lived reptiles that can live over 120 years. They have extraordinary memories and are able to know exactly where they are due to an internal map (which is why you should never relocate them). Their populations have been hurt by illegal pet trade. - Alex Edwards
